subject: english
title: PSLE English — Synthesis & Transformation Tricks (10 marks)

stats.headline: Synthesis = 10 marks. PSLE rotates the same 4 grammar patterns every year. Learn the keyword-to-pattern map and the most common traps — full marks become straightforward.

keyConcepts.title: Welcome — Synthesis & Transformation (10 marks)
keyConcepts.body: Synthesis asks you to REWRITE a sentence using a given keyword, keeping the meaning. 8 patterns cover almost every PSLE question in the bank. The hardest part isn't grammar — it's not changing meaning, tense, or adding info.
bullets: **A: Concession** — Although / Despite / In spite of / No matter
**B: Cause** — Because / Due to / Owing to / On account of
**C: Condition** — If / Unless / Only with
**D: Reported Speech** — direct ↔ indirect, with backshift
**E: Preference** — Would rather / Prefer / Preference for
**F: Participle (Having)** — Having + V-ed, joining two actions
**G: Inclusion / Correlative** — Both…and / Either…or / Neither…nor / Not only…but also
**H: Relative Clause** — who / whom / which / whose

**The 3 rules of synthesis:**
① Don't change the meaning. ② Don't change the tense. ③ Don't add info.
Pattern **A: Concession** — Although / Despite / In spite of / No matter.
Pattern **B: Cause** — Because / Due to / Owing to / On account of.
Pattern **C: Condition** — If / Unless / Only with.
Pattern **D: Reported Speech** — direct ↔ indirect, with backshift.
Pattern **E: Preference** — Would rather / Prefer / Preference for.
Pattern **F: Participle (Having)** — Having + V-ed, joining two actions.
Pattern **G: Inclusion / Correlative** — Both…and / Either…or / Neither…nor / Not only…but also.
Pattern **H: Relative Clause** — who / whom / which / whose.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern A — Concession (Although / Despite)
keyConcepts.body: Two contrasting ideas. The PSLE trap: students mix up the grammar after Although vs Despite.
bullets:
**Although + CLAUSE** (subject + verb)
✓ Although **he was tired**, he kept working.

**Despite / In spite of + NOUN PHRASE**
✓ Despite **his tiredness**, he kept working.
✗ Despite **he was tired**, he kept working.

**Conversion** (Although → Despite): verb → noun/-ing, drop the subject.
"Although it was raining" → "Despite the rain."

**Common mistakes** (from real student work)
✗ Changing the original word — wrote "homework" when the source said "work". Synthesis is **all or nothing**: any word swap = 0 marks for that sentence.
✗ Leaving "No matter" questions blank because the structure feels foreign — "No matter how hard I tried…" follows the same pattern as Although, just with a bigger setup.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern B — Cause (Because / Due to)
keyConcepts.body: Same clause-vs-noun split as Concession. PSLE drills both because they share the conversion trick.
bullets:
**Because / Since / As + CLAUSE**
✓ Because **the bus broke down**, we were late.

**Due to / Owing to + NOUN PHRASE**
✓ Due to **the bus breakdown**, we were late.
✗ Due to **the bus broke down**, we were late.

**Conversion** (Because → Due to): same trick — verb → noun, drop subject.
"Because she was sick" → "Due to her illness."

**Common mistakes** (from real student work)
✗ Dropping a modifier — "Sam fell ill **suddenly**" must become "sudden illness", not just "illness". Skipping "sudden" = 0 marks.
✗ Spelling slips on the derived noun: "indecision" not "indecesion". The noun has to be a real word.
✗ Repeating the subject ("Sam") when the rewrite has already named them — use the pronoun ("he").

interactiveQuiz.label: Concession — Although → Despite
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Although the weather was bad, the football match continued.
Rewrite using "Despite". Which is correct?
options: Despite the weather was bad, the football match continued.
Despite the bad weather, the football match continued.
Despite the football match, the weather was bad.
Despite of the bad weather, the football match continued.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Despite takes a NOUN PHRASE. "The weather was bad" (clause) becomes "the bad weather". Note: "Despite of" is always wrong — just "Despite".

interactiveQuiz.label: Cause — Because → Due to
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Because the road was flooded, the bus could not pass.
Rewrite using "Due to". Which is correct?
options: Due to the road was flooded, the bus could not pass.
Due to the flooded road, the bus could not pass.
Due to that the road was flooded, the bus could not pass.
Due to flooding the road, the bus could not pass.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Due to + noun phrase. "The road was flooded" → "the flooded road". Option 4 changes the meaning ("flooding the road" implies someone is doing the flooding).

keyConcepts.title: Pattern C — Condition (If / Unless)
keyConcepts.body: Unless = "if not". When converting If → Unless, you must NEGATE the if-clause. Double negatives = the #1 trap.
bullets:
**If + present, will + verb**
✓ If you hurry, you will catch the bus.

**Unless = If…not** — flip the negation!
✓ Unless you hurry, you will miss the bus.   (= If you don't hurry…)
✗ Unless you don't hurry, you will catch the bus.   (double negative)

**Conversion** (If → Unless): drop "not" if there is one, add "not" if there isn't.
"If you don't study, you will fail" → "Unless you study, you will fail."

**Common mistakes** (from real student work)
✗ Keeping the "not" after Unless → double negative.
"If we do **not** leave now, we will be late" → "Unless we leave now" (NOT "Unless we **do not** leave").
✗ Missing articles when rewriting: "we will start lesson" → should be "the lesson". Articles count.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern D — Reported Speech (Direct ↔ Indirect)
keyConcepts.body: Convert direct speech (with quotes) to indirect (no quotes). Headline rule: BACKSHIFT — when the reporting verb is past (said / told / asked), every tense moves one step further back. The biggest trap: past → **past perfect (had + past participle)**.
bullets:
**Tense backshift table** (when reporting verb is past)
-- present (am/is/are) → past (was/were)
-- present continuous (am eating) → past continuous (was eating)
-- **past (broke / went) → past perfect (had broken / had gone)**  ⚠️ the past-past trap
-- present perfect (has done) → past perfect (had done)
-- will → would   ·   can → could   ·   may → might

**⚠️ The past-past trap** (most-missed)
Direct: "I **broke** the window," John said.
✗ John said that he **broke** the window.
✓ John said that he **had broken** the window.
Past + past = ambiguous time order. Use past perfect for the earlier event.

**Pronoun & time/place shifts**
-- I → he/she   ·   you → I/me   ·   we → they   ·   my → his/her
-- today → that day   ·   **yesterday → the day before**
-- tomorrow → the next day   ·   now → then
-- here → there   ·   this → that   ·   these → those

**Questions** — use `asked if / whether`; drop "do/did"; statement order; no question mark.
-- "Where do you live?" he asked. → He asked where I **lived**.
-- "Are you coming?" she asked. → She asked **if** I **was** coming.

**Commands** — use `told [person] to + verb`.
-- "Sit down!" the teacher said. → The teacher **told** us **to sit down**.

**Common mistakes** (from real student work)
✗ Using "they" / vague pronouns when the original speaker addressed a specific person — that changes the meaning. Match the original referent exactly.
✗ Forgetting the verb backshift — "saw" must become "had seen" when the reporting verb is past.
✗ Lowercase "if" / "i" at the start of the rewrite. Capitalisation counts.
✗ Synonym swaps for time/place words — "the day before" is not the same as the expected "the previous day". Use the EXACT shift words from the table.

interactiveQuiz.label: Unless negation trap
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: If you don't wear a raincoat, you will get wet.
Rewrite starting with "Unless". Which is correct?
options: Unless you don't wear a raincoat, you will get wet.
Unless you wear a raincoat, you will not get wet.
Unless you wear a raincoat, you will get wet.
Unless you wear a raincoat, you will be wet.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "Unless" already means "if not", so DROP the "don't" when converting. "If you don't wear" → "Unless you wear". The main clause stays the same — don't add a second negative.

interactiveQuiz.label: Past-past trap
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Direct: "I lost my wallet yesterday," Tom said.
Convert to reported speech. Which is correct?
options: Tom said that he lost his wallet yesterday.
Tom said that he had lost his wallet the day before.
Tom said that he has lost his wallet yesterday.
Tom said that he had lost his wallet yesterday.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Two backshifts must happen together: the verb "lost" (simple past) → "had lost" (past perfect), AND the time word "yesterday" → "the day before". Option 1 misses both. Options 3 and 4 catch one but not the other.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern E — Preference (Would rather / Prefer)
keyConcepts.body: Two activities the speaker compares; one is preferred. Three surface forms tested most: "would rather", "prefer", and the noun "preference for".
bullets:
**Would rather + bare verb + than + bare verb**
✓ Alice would rather **read books** than **watch TV**.
✗ Alice would rather **read books** than **to watch TV**.   (no "to")

**Prefer + V-ing to V-ing**  /  **Prefer + noun to noun**
✓ My grandparents prefer **visiting** local attractions **to travelling** overseas.

**Have a preference for X over Y**
✓ My grandparents have a **preference for** visiting local attractions **over** travelling overseas.
✗ "preference to travelling" — wrong preposition. "over", not "to".

keyConcepts.title: Pattern F — Participle (Having + V-ed)
keyConcepts.body: Joins TWO actions where the first finishes before the second starts. "Having" + past participle replaces the first sentence's subject + verb. The original subject moves to the SECOND clause.
bullets:
**Having + past participle, + new subject + verb**
✓ Mary **finished** her homework. She went out to play.
→ **Having finished** her homework, **Mary** went out to play.

**Not having** (negative form)
✓ Amy **had not baked** cookies before. She burnt all of them.
→ **Not having baked** cookies before, **Amy** burnt all of them.

**Two checkpoints** before submitting:
① Past participle is correct (drew → drawn, eat → eaten, write → written).
② The "his / her / their" possessives match the original — DON'T change them.

**Common mistakes** (from real student work)
✗ Changing "his" / "her" / "their" to "a" or "the" — meaning shift = 0 marks.
✗ Using the wrong past participle (drewn, eaten/ate confusion). Common one: "drawn", not "drew".
✗ Repeating the subject in BOTH clauses ("Having finished her homework, **she** Mary went…"). Pick one — usually the proper noun in the second clause.

interactiveQuiz.label: Preference — would rather
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Rani enjoys cycling. She does not enjoy swimming.
Rewrite using "would rather". Which is correct?
options: Rani would rather cycle than to swim.
Rani would rather cycling than swimming.
Rani would rather cycle than swim.
Rani prefers cycle than swim.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "Would rather" is followed by the BARE infinitive (no "to"). Both halves use the same form — cycle / swim. Option 1 adds "to" by mistake; option 2 uses gerunds (would rather doesn't take -ing); option 4 confuses two patterns.

interactiveQuiz.label: Participle — Having
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: John drew a picture of his dog. Then he framed it up.
Rewrite starting with "Having". Which is correct?
options: Having drew a picture of his dog, John framed it up.
Having drawn a picture of a dog, John framed it up.
Having drawn a picture of his dog, John framed it up.
John drew a picture of his dog, having framed it up.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Two checks. Verb form: "drawn" (past participle), not "drew" — that rules out option 1. Possessive: "his dog" stays "his dog"; option 2 changed it to "a" which changes the meaning. Option 4 puts "having framed" on the wrong action.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern G — Inclusion (Both / Either / Neither / Not only)
keyConcepts.body: Pairs of conjunctions that link two equal parts. The trap is what comes AFTER the second half — verb agreement and word order shift depending on the pair.
bullets:
**Both X and Y** — both must be the same kind (noun + noun, verb + verb).
✓ **Both** Ali **and** Lily **like** to hike at Bukit Timah Hill.

**Either X or Y**  /  **Neither X nor Y**
✓ We can **either** play soccer **or** go to the library.
✓ **Neither** Ali **nor** Lily likes swimming.

**Not only X but (also) Y** — INVERSION after "Not only" at sentence start.
✓ **Not only does** Sarah **sing** beautifully, **but she also** plays the piano well.
✗ "Not only Sarah sings beautifully, but she plays the piano." (no inversion = wrong)

**X or Y** (warning + consequence form):
✓ Hand in your assignment on time **or** your marks will be deducted.

**The "Not only" inversion formula** (must be memorised):
**Not only + DOES / DID / HAS + Subject + BASE VERB**
✓ Not only **does** Sarah **sing** beautifully, but she also plays the piano well.
✓ Not only **did** Ali **win** the race, but he also broke the record.
✓ Not only **has** Mark **finished** his work, but he has also helped others.
Notice: the verb after the subject is in BASE form (sing, win) — the tense is carried by the helper (does, did, has).

**Common mistake**
✗ Forgetting the inversion: "Not only Sarah sings beautifully…" — synthesis is **all-or-nothing**, the whole rewrite is marked wrong without "does".

keyConcepts.title: Pattern H — Relative Clause (who / whom / which / whose)
keyConcepts.body: Joins two sentences where the second describes a noun in the first. The relative pronoun depends on what it replaces, AND where you put it.
bullets:
**who** = subject (people). **whom** = object (people).
Quick test:
– If you could replace it with **he / she / they** → use **who**.
– If you could replace it with **him / her / them** → use **whom**.

**which** = things. **whose** = possessive (people or things).
✓ My mother bought a new blender **which can** make fruit juices.
✓ The boy **whose** dog was lost is my classmate.

**POSITION RULE — the relative pronoun must sit RIGHT AFTER the noun it describes.**
Original: We admire Mr Kwan. He is our local football player.
✓ **Mr Kwan, whom we admire,** is our local football player.
✗ Mr Kwan is our local football player, **whom we admire**.   (whom is detached from Mr Kwan)
Why it's wrong: the relative clause has to touch the noun. If "whom we admire" sits at the end, the reader can't tell which noun it describes — it looks like it's describing "football player".

**Defining vs non-defining clauses — the comma rule:**
– **Defining clause = essential info, NO commas.** The clause picks out WHICH one you mean. Without it the sentence doesn't make sense.
✓ The robber **who wore a black mask** was caught.
(There were several robbers; "who wore a black mask" tells us which one. No commas.)
– **Non-defining clause = extra info, USES commas.** The clause adds detail you could remove and the sentence still makes sense.
✓ Mr Kwan, **whom we admire**, is our local football player.
(We already know it's Mr Kwan; "whom we admire" is extra info. Commas required on BOTH sides.)
Quick test: read the sentence without the clause. If the meaning still holds → non-defining, comma it. If the meaning breaks → defining, no commas.

**Common mistakes** (from real student work)
✗ Placing the relative clause far from the noun — "Mr Kwan is our football player, whom we admire" puts "whom" miles from "Mr Kwan". Synthesis is **all-or-nothing**: position wrong = 0 marks.
✗ Missing commas on a non-defining clause — both commas are required, one on each side.

interactiveQuiz.label: Inclusion — Not only
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Sarah sings beautifully. She plays the piano well.
Rewrite starting with "Not only". Which is correct?
options: Not only Sarah sings beautifully, but she also plays the piano well.
Not only does Sarah sings beautifully, but she also plays the piano well.
Not only does Sarah sing beautifully, but she also plays the piano well.
Sarah not only sings beautifully and she plays the piano well.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "Not only" at the start FORCES inversion: "does + Sarah + BASE verb". Option 1 has no inversion; option 2 keeps "sings" (after "does" the verb must be in base form); option 4 rearranges and drops "but also". Option 3 inverts correctly and keeps both halves balanced with "but she also".

interactiveQuiz.label: Relative — who vs whom
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: We admire Mr Kwan. He is our local football player.
Rewrite using "whom". Which is correct?
options: Mr Kwan, whom we admire, is our local football player.
Mr Kwan whom we admire is our local football player.
Mr Kwan, whom is our local football player, we admire.
We admire Mr Kwan, whom is our local football player.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "whom" is the OBJECT of "admire" (we admire him → whom). The non-defining clause "whom we admire" needs commas. Options 2 and 3/4 either miss the commas or use "whom" as a subject ("whom is" is always wrong — subject pronoun should be "who").

keyConcepts.title: Start practising!
keyConcepts.body: Mixed practice drawn from PSLE 2016-2025 Synthesis & Transformation. Covers all 8 patterns from the slides. Trains the reflex of checking "same tense, same info, same meaning" on every rewrite.
bullets: **15 questions** — 8 patterns
Wrong answers auto-saved, reviewed in 1 week
AI marks word-for-word — partial credit if you almost got it but missed tense/info
You'll tackle **15 questions** — a mix across the 8 patterns from this class.
Any Wrong answers are auto-saved, and reviewed in 1 week.
The AI marks your answers word-for-word — giving partial credit if you almost got it but missed the tense/info.

keyWords.definition: Two contrasting ideas joined: 'tired BUT kept going'. Keywords: Although + clause / Despite + noun phrase / In spite of + noun phrase. The grammar split (clause vs noun phrase) is the most common trap.
Same grammar split as concession. Because/Since/As take a clause (subject + verb). Due to / Owing to take a noun phrase. Convert verb → noun + drop subject.
Means 'if...not'. When converting If → Unless, NEGATE the if-clause (drop 'not' or add 'not'). Double negatives are the #1 trap.

In reported speech, when the reporting verb is past, every tense shifts one step further back: present → past, past → past perfect (had + past participle). Pronouns and time/place words shift too: yesterday → the day before, here → there.
Compares two activities. Followed by BARE verbs: 'would rather X than Y' (no 'to' before Y). 'Prefer' uses gerunds: 'prefer V-ing to V-ing'. 'Preference' uses 'for X over Y'.

Joins two actions where the first finishes before the second starts. Replaces 'Subject + did X. Then subject did Y' with 'Having + V-ed X, subject did Y'. Possessives in the original (his / her / their) must stay unchanged.
When 'Not only' starts the sentence, the verb INVERTS: 'Not only DOES Sarah sing beautifully, but she also plays the piano well'. Forgetting the 'does / did / has' costs 1 mark.
who = subject (replaces he/she/they) — 'who teaches us'. whom = object (replaces him/her/them) — 'whom we admire'. Test: if you could substitute 'he/she' use 'who'; if 'him/her' use 'whom'.
① Don't change the tense. ② Don't add info. ③ Don't change the meaning. Every rewrite must pass all three checks before you submit.

subTopics.label: Concession (Although/Despite)
subTopics.description: Two contrasting ideas. Although + clause vs Despite/In spite of + noun phrase. Conversion trick: verb → noun/-ing form, drop subject. PSLE's most-tested pattern.

subTopics.label: Cause (Because/Due to)
subTopics.description: Same grammar split as concession. Because/Since/As + clause; Due to/Owing to + noun phrase. Same conversion technique applies.

subTopics.label: Condition (If/Unless)
subTopics.description: Unless = if + not. When converting If → Unless, you must NEGATE the if-clause. Double-negative trap is common.

subTopics.label: Reported Speech (backshift)
subTopics.description: Direct ↔ indirect. Backshift tenses one step (present → past, past → past perfect). Also shift pronouns and time words (yesterday → the day before). Past-past trap is the headline mistake.

subTopics.label: Preference (Would rather / Prefer)
subTopics.description: Compare two activities; one is preferred. Would rather + bare verb + than + bare verb. Prefer + V-ing to V-ing. Preference + for X over Y. Trap: adding 'to' after 'than'.

subTopics.label: Participle (Having + V-ed)
subTopics.description: Joins two actions where the first finishes before the second starts. Having + past participle replaces the first sentence's subject + verb. The original subject moves to the second clause. Keep his/her possessives intact.

subTopics.label: Inclusion (Both / Either / Neither / Not only)
subTopics.description: Pair conjunctions linking two equal parts. Headline trap: 'Not only' at sentence start FORCES inversion ('Not only DOES Sarah sing'). 'Or' as warning + consequence is also tested.

subTopics.label: Relative Clause (who / whom / which / whose)
subTopics.description: Joins two sentences where the second describes a noun in the first. who = subject (people), whom = object (people), which = things, whose = possessive. Non-defining clauses need commas.
